reason: The item cleanly targets a single expert judgment: separating thermal runaway occurrence/severity from vent-gas flammability hazard in the presence of a non-monotonic SOC dependence and a threshold near 50% SOC. Correct options are tightly bounded to the provided FHI ordering and threshold behavior, while decoys mostly fail via single, realistic over-inferences (e.g., “no TR implies no gas hazard” or “lower SOC always safer”). Minor weakness is that some options use absolute language that can be spotted as risky, slightly reducing neutrality and allowing modest test-wise elimination.

The item cleanly targets a boundary-aware engineering judgment: flammability hazard is multi-metric and non-monotonic with SOC, requiring distinct worst-case identification for ignition vs deflagration/overall hazard. It has strong discriminative power because it penalizes the common heuristic that “highest SOC is always worst,” yet remains answerable by experts using the provided metric distinctions. Minor risk remains that as a short-answer prompt, scoring reliability depends on how strictly the rubric enforces mentioning both metrics and their associated SOC worst cases.
